nativeRange.habitat: Shallow Caribbean reef tops, fore-reef terraces, and back-reef areas with moderate to strong water movement and good sunlight; commonly forms large bushy colonies on hard substrate

decorPreferences: live rock anchor point in middle to upper aquascape
epoxy or two-part putty for base attachment to rockwork
adequate spacing from neighboring corals and anemones
open water column position with consistent moderate-to-high flow
moderate to bright reef-capable lighting overhead

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae — the source of its energy under reef-capable lighting. Supplemental feeding is generally optional rather than mandatory; light feeding several times per week with phytoplankton, live or frozen baby brine shrimp nauplii, rotifers, Marine Snow, or commercial coral foods has been reported to improve growth and polyp extension. Trace-element supplementation (iodine, calcium, strontium) is commonly recommended alongside regular partial water changes.

avoidWith: aggressive corals placed within sweeping range
low-flow systems prone to detritus buildup on branches
urchins or large hermit crabs that climb and damage branches

summary: The Purple Candelabra Gorgonian (Plexaura flexuosa) is a Caribbean reef coral widely regarded as producing some of the most vivid purple coloration seen in any gorgonian, its smooth branches covered in hundreds of small, fuzzy tan polyps. It is generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly photosynthetic gorgonians available to reef keepers.
careNotes:
The Purple Candelabra Gorgonian is generally considered one of the easier photosynthetic gorgonians for home aquarists, commonly described as fairly forgiving when it comes to water chemistry as long as lighting and flow are appropriate. The scientific name Plexaura flexuosa remains the most widely used in aquaculture and trade, though it is also sold under the older name Eunicea flexuosa.

Key care requirements:

Lighting: Moderate to moderately high reef-capable lighting (approximately 100–200 PAR) for 8–12 hours daily is generally advised. The species is photosynthetic via zooxanthellae (symbiotic algae living within its tissues) and derives most of its energy from light. Excessively intense lighting has been reported to cause discoloration; pale specimens can often be kept at lower intensities.

Flow: Moderate to moderately high, variable (non-laminar) flow is generally considered essential. The branches should be stirring slightly but not blown over. Too little flow tends to allow detritus, algae, and cyanobacteria to colonize the branches and skeleton; too much flow tends to retract the polyps and slow growth. Polyp extension is the key indicator that flow is appropriate.

Placement: Middle to upper portions of the aquascape generally work best — high enough for good light exposure but anchored securely in the rockwork. The base should be glued or epoxy-mounted to live rock, since gorgonians do not encrust their base onto substrate the way many other corals do.

Feeding: Photosynthetic and self-sustaining under appropriate lighting; supplemental feeding is generally optional rather than mandatory, unlike non-photosynthetic gorgonians that depend on direct feeding to survive. Light feeding several times per week with phytoplankton, live or frozen baby brine shrimp nauplii, or commercial coral foods has been reported to improve growth and color.

Water chemistry: Generally regarded as forgiving — temperature 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.026, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 350–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm. Some sources have noted the species tolerates and may even prefer slightly elevated nutrient conditions (nitrate 5–15 ppm, phosphate 0.05–0.15 ppm) compared to ultra-low-nutrient systems focused on stony corals.

Algae and cyanobacteria control: Colonization of branches by film algae or cyanobacteria (a type of photosynthetic bacteria that can smother coral tissue) is the most commonly cited cause of decline. Maintaining vigorous flow around the colony, keeping nutrient levels balanced, and ensuring appropriate lighting all reduce this risk.

Mounting: The base should be anchored to live rock with aquarium-safe epoxy or two-part putty. Branches tend to be flexible rather than brittle, but careful handling during placement is still advisable.

Use as seahorse hitching post: Often recommended specifically for seahorse displays as a natural-looking 'hitching post' — its branching structure and gentle movement make it a well-suited anchor species for Hippocampus seahorses.

Growth: Reported as fast-growing once established, with wild colonies reaching several feet tall in large bushes. Hobby-traded specimens are typically 8–25 cm at point of sale.
breedingNotes: Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural Caribbean reefs but is not commonly attempted in home aquariums. Fragmentation — cutting branches and reattaching them to frag plugs or live rock with aquarium-safe epoxy — is the primary captive propagation method and is reported to be straightforward. Branches tend to re-establish readily on new substrate when water quality and flow are adequate.

primary.name: Pacific East Aquaculture – Purple Candelabra Photosynthetic Gorgonian
primary.notes: Used as primary in lieu of World Wide Corals, which has no product page for this species (404 on direct fetch and no results in catalog search). Pacific East is a long-running US aquaculture specialty source with detailed parameter listings. Provided scientific name Plexaura flexuosa, temperature 24–26°C, salinity 1.026 SG, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8 dKH, calcium 440 ppm, magnesium 1320 ppm, NO3 5–15 ppm, PO4 0.05–0.15 ppm, medium-high lighting, medium-high flow, low difficulty / beginner, reef safe, peaceful, photosynthetic feeding.

additional.name: ND Aquaculture – Purple Candelabra Gorgonian
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Plexaura flexuosa), Caribbean / Florida Keys native range, water parameters (SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, 70–78°F, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, Ca 350–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm), moderate-to-higher reef-capable lighting (8–12 h), moderate-to-higher flow, hardy/beginner care level, reef safe, non-aggressive temperament, and explicit 'perfect hitching post for seahorses' note. Confirmed the species is photosynthetic and does not require supplemental feeding.

additional.name: Seahorse Savvy – Purple Candelabra Gorgonian (Photosynthetic)
additional.notes: Independent confirmation of scientific name Plexaura flexuosa, Caribbean/Florida Keys/South Florida native range, hardy reef-safe status, suitability as a seahorse hitching post, beige-to-light polyps against purple branches morphology.

additional.name: Reef Builders – Photosynthetic Gorgonians for the Home Aquaria
additional.notes: Cross-checked husbandry guidance: 'strong, variable flow and moderate to high lighting' under metal halide, T5, or LED. Confirms 'strongest purple coloration of any gorgonians' and fast growth (wild colonies several feet tall in large bushes). Author calls it their personal favorite photosynthetic gorgonian.

additional.name: WoRMS – Plexaura flexuosa (synonymy reference)
additional.notes: Taxonomy-only reference. WoRMS treats Plexaura flexuosa Lamouroux, 1821 as a junior synonym of the currently accepted Eunicea flexuosa (Lamouroux, 1821); family Plexauridae, order Malacalcyonacea. Not used for husbandry. The trade name and scientificName in this entry intentionally retain 'Plexaura flexuosa' to match how every reviewed aquaculture source currently sells and labels the species.
